name: happy-path-dashboard-design
description: User asks to build a data dashboard component. Skill should state design choices first, use 4px grid, choose a depth strategy, and produce clean CSS/HTML.
prompt: Build me a stats dashboard card component that shows KPIs like revenue, active users, and conversion rate. Use React with inline styles or CSS modules.
expectations: Response states design choices (direction, depth strategy, spacing) BEFORE writing any code
All spacing values in the generated code are multiples of 4 (e.g., 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 32, 48) — no odd values like 5, 7, 10, 14, 15, 25
Code uses exactly ONE depth strategy throughout — either borders-only OR subtle shadows OR layered shadows, never mixing approaches
Typography for numeric/data values uses monospace or tabular-nums (font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums or font-family referencing a monospace font)
Response offers to save patterns to Subcog after completing the component
Border radius values are consistent across all elements — not randomly mixed sizes

name: scope-detection-marketing-redirect
description: User asks to build a landing page. Skill should redirect to /frontend-design.
prompt: I need a beautiful landing page for my new SaaS product — hero section, feature grid, testimonials, and a CTA. Can you design it?
expectations: Response redirects the user to /frontend-design (mentions the command or skill by name)
Response does NOT produce a full landing page implementation with hero/CTA sections
Response explains that aesth is for dashboards/apps/tools, not marketing sites
Response does NOT announce 'I am checking scope' or narrate the redirection decision process

name: spacing-validation-off-grid
description: User provides code with off-grid spacing. Skill should identify violations and fix them to 4px multiples.
expectations: Response identifies that 15px, 22px, 14px, 7px, 9px, 5px, 25px, and 11px are not on a 4px grid
Corrected values are all multiples of 4 (e.g., 16px instead of 15px, 24px instead of 22px or 25px, 12px instead of 11px)
Response explains WHY grid alignment matters (system consistency, intentional spacing)
Corrected padding values maintain symmetry unless there is explicit justification for asymmetry
Response does NOT just say 'spacing looks off' — it gives specific corrected values for each property

name: anti-pattern-detection
description: User provides code with multiple anti-patterns. Skill should flag all of them specifically.
expectations: Response flags the dramatic/heavy drop shadow (0 20px 60px with 0.5 opacity) as an anti-pattern
Response flags the decorative gradient (linear-gradient) as an anti-pattern
Response flags the multiple accent colors (ff6b6b, 00d2ff, f7971e) as an anti-pattern — color should communicate meaning, not decorate
Response flags the thick decorative border (3px solid) as an anti-pattern
Response flags the excessive padding (64px) as an anti-pattern (>48px margins/padding)
Response flags the large border-radius (24px) as potentially inappropriate for a card element

name: design-direction-suggestion-fintech
description: User describes a fintech product. Skill should suggest an appropriate direction and ask for confirmation.
prompt: I'm building a portfolio management dashboard for a fintech startup. Users track investments, view account balances, and analyze market trends. What direction should we take?
expectations: Response suggests a direction that emphasizes trust, precision, or sophistication — NOT warmth/playfulness
Response recommends tight/dense spacing appropriate for data-heavy financial interfaces
Response recommends monospace or tabular-nums for financial figures
Response explicitly asks for user confirmation before proceeding (suggest+ask pattern)
Response recommends a borders-only or subtle-shadows depth strategy — NOT layered/dramatic shadows for a financial tool
Response does NOT announce 'I am in ESTABLISH MODE' or narrate internal process

name: communication-style-invisible
description: Skill should work invisibly without narrating its process or announcing modes.
prompt: Build me a settings page layout with a sidebar navigation and a content area. Use Tailwind CSS.
expectations: Response does NOT contain phrases like 'I'm in ESTABLISH MODE' or 'Let me check Subcog' or 'Entering design mode'
Response does NOT narrate steps like 'First I will query memory' or 'Now I am applying design principles'
Response states design choices (direction, depth, spacing) directly without meta-commentary about the process
Response produces actual code for the settings page layout, not just a plan or description

name: typography-hierarchy-data-table
description: User asks for a data table component. Skill should use proper typography hierarchy with monospace for data.
prompt: Build me a data table component for displaying server metrics — hostname, CPU usage, memory, disk, and status. React component please.
expectations: Numeric data columns (CPU %, memory, disk) use monospace font or font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums so digits align vertically
Column headers have visually distinct weight or size from table body text (typography hierarchy)
Status column uses color to communicate meaning (e.g., green for healthy, red for critical) rather than decorative color
All spacing/padding values in the table are multiples of 4px
Response states design choices (direction, depth, spacing) before the code

name: memory-persistence-offer
description: After building a component, skill should offer to save patterns to Subcog.
prompt: Build me a notification toast component with success, warning, and error variants. Keep it minimal.
prompt_suffix: After the skill produces the component, check the tail of the response.
expectations: Response includes an offer to save patterns to Subcog (mentions 'Subcog', 'save', or 'capture' in context of persisting the design)
The save offer comes AFTER the component code, not before
The offer specifically mentions saving patterns, direction, or design tokens — not just generic 'save your work'
Color is used meaningfully in the variants (success=green-ish, error=red-ish, warning=amber/yellow-ish) rather than arbitrary decorative colors
